question: Why is women's work participation so low?

theme.label: Society, work, education, and services
readerQuestions: What basic capability is being measured?
How many people does the percentage represent?
Is this access, usage, quality, or outcome?
Which caveat stops the chart from being overclaimed?

visualPlan.title: Labour Force Participation Rate — female (15+)
visualPlan.recommendedVisualization: single-series line chart with latest value and first-to-latest change

plannedCharts.title: Women's participation in the labour force
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The headline: how many working-age women are in the labour force.

plannedCharts.title: The gap between men and women at work
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The chasm is the story: men's participation is roughly double women's.

plannedCharts.title: The long view: decline, then recovery
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Female participation fell for years before the recent turnaround.

plannedCharts.title: The puzzle: urban women work less
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Counter to intuition, rural female participation is higher than urban.

plannedCharts.title: India near the bottom of the world
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: How far below its neighbours India sits on women's work.

plannedCharts.title: Women actually working vs men
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Not just looking for work — actually employed.

plannedCharts.title: Young women struggle most to find work
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Where the barrier bites hardest: young women entering the market.

plannedCharts.title: The pay gap in regular jobs
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: Even in regular salaried work, women earn less.

plannedCharts.title: Rural wages: men vs women
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The pay gap shows up in daily rural wages too.

plannedCharts.title: The recent surge, month by month
plannedCharts.whyThisChart: The fast recent rise in female participation, at monthly frequency.

selectionRules: Start from the page question, not from the indicator name.
Choose the smallest set of visuals that explains scale, trend, structure, comparison, and caveat.
Prefer mobile-friendly charts: line charts with sparse ticks, horizontal bars, share strips, pyramids only when essential, and no dense tables.
Use one strong pull quote only when the evidence supports it with a number.
Add glossary blocks for technical terms that normal readers may not know.
caveats: Use only figures present in lockedNumbers or sourceSummaries.
If a related theme indicator is present, you may use it for context, but do not pretend it is the main answer.
If source coverage is thin, say so directly.
Do not compare states, districts, or cities unless the evidence packet contains those geographies.
forbiddenClaims: Do not infer causality from a trend unless the evidence explicitly supports it.
Do not cite a source that is not present in sourceSummaries.
